raw_response_text:
<features>
- pattern: parallel wavy lines
- texture: granular/sandy texture
- color: reddish-brown/tan
</features>
<kb>
- IF [pattern = parallel straight lines OR parallel wavy lines] THEN [17]
- IF [pattern = irregular holes OR broken ground] THEN [32]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- The rule for class 17 is satisfied because the image displays a clear pattern of parallel wavy lines across the surface.
</rule_check>

<response>17</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- surface: asphalt/pavement
- features: holes/depressions in the surface
- features: water-filled puddles within the holes
</features>
<kb>
- IF [surface = parallel lines/slats/planks] THEN [17]
- IF [surface = asphalt/pavement AND features = holes/puddles] THEN [32]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 32 fires because the image shows an asphalt surface with holes containing water-filled puddles, matching the pattern seen in the support images for class 32.
</rule_check>

<response>32</response>

Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>

referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

- features: holes/depressions in the surface
